affected_groups: future cohorts under any reformed system
content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue the structural reform most likely to reduce inequality of opportunity even though comparable reforms elsewhere have proven politically reversible, or favor more durable but partial measures that leave the core sorting mechanism intact?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence (e.g., political-economy analysis of coalition durability, longitudinal tracking of Polish-style reversals) can clarify the probability and mechanisms of reversal and help estimate expected net benefit under uncertainty, but it cannot determine whether short-term equity gains are worth accepting a nontrivial risk of future rollback — this is a judgment about how to weigh present versus future/uncertain benefits, not a fact evidence can settle.
content.tension: Delaying the selection age to 14-16 (T2) or piloting comprehensive de-tracking (T6) most directly targets the mechanism most strongly linked to inequality of opportunity, but comparable structural reforms elsewhere (Poland) were substantially reversed despite measured success, and Hungary's founding 1989-90 coalition-based settlement makes any structural change highly vulnerable to symbolic mobilization. Choosing bold structural reform risks investing significant political and administrative capital in a change that may not survive a subsequent political cycle, while choosing incremental reform (T3, T4, T5) risks perpetuating the core sorting mechanism indefinitely.
content.title: Structural transformation versus institutional durability
value_poles: institutional stability and durability of reform
